feedback.text: Hello, since the early days of the introduction of the internet we have seen a lot of potential in digital distribution and services similar to that. A lot of these opportunities have been beneficial towards producers and customers alike. Unfortunately some also have a negative impact and are actively used to take or slowly strip away basic customer rights with little to no regulation. For that I have two concerns that I address and explain below. Lost Media: I take my personal experience as an example. I bought access for the live service game "Dreadnought" [2017], which seemingly was not financially viable so the producer understandably took it down in 2023. Unfortunately that rendered the game unplayable and inaccessible for everyone who bought access, as servers were shut down needed for the functionability. Similar case "BattleForge" [2009], which had a singleplayer mode attached to it, but can not be accessed anymore due to online requirements shut down in 2013. The physical disc I bought is completely useless for that matter. Even more media is at risk of becoming so-called "lost media" as more and more games are tied to online functionality to gather end user data and influencing customer behaviour. Titles without the need for online functionality like singleplayer games, for example Assassins Creed 2 [2009] are bound to always-online requirements and ingame microtransactions, artificially tying those products life cycles to financial viability or even a mere subscriber-licenses, for which access can be revoked at any time and for any reason. Some big game publishers like Ubisoft have included in their EULA in 2025 by quote You and UBISOFT (or its licensors) may terminate this EULA, at any time, for any reason. and demanding of customers that Upon termination for any reason, You must immediately uninstall the Product and destroy all copies of the Product in Your possession., which is not favourable nor fair to the customers who bought the product. The European Citizens' Initiative "Stop Killing Games" provides further examples on bad practices of the larger videogame industry to control customer behaviour, spending and potentially ways on how to fix it. Payment processors: Another issue is the questionable impact of private payment providers such as PayPal, Visa/Mastercard, Stripe and similar, who uphold the majority share of the service of processing payments worldwide. These payment providers recently threatened videogame platforms such as Steam or Itch.io with a withdrawal of their services if they do not follow content guidelines set by those providers themselves, based on moral judgement and brand protection. Due to that, those game platforms mentioned, had to discontinue the distribution of tens of thousands of games that were legally allowed, but are seen as a brand risk by the payment processors who demanded their takedown and now can no longer be bought and accessed at all. That is a massive overreach in responsibilities by those payment processors denying legal business, ruining the income and freedom of expression for possibly tens of thousands, often independent and small developers and individuals. It also serves as a method of control which products are allowed to be bought or not on a worldwide scale, including EU citizens. I condemn such actions of moral censorship and would like this behaviour, if possible, to be included and prohibited in the Digital Fairness Act or be addressed in other avenues, as those companies should not have a say in what contents are distributed on third-party platforms, as long as those contents are within legal limits. Conclusion: Thank you for reading my concerns. It is in my hopes and desire that the EU commission sees these overreaches in control and censorship as obvious anti-consumer behaviour and finds a constructive solution concerning those bad business practices for its citizens and member states. Best regards to you.
